items.q: Why does water have a bent shape rather than linear?
items.correct: Oxygen has two bonding pairs and two lone pairs, giving a tetrahedral electron geometry but a bent molecular shape.
items.misconception: Water is bent because the hydrogens repel each other and push apart.
items.random_wrong: Water is bent because oxygen is heavier than hydrogen and weighs the middle down.
items.why: Students see H atoms at the ends and intuit H-H repulsion, missing that the bend is caused by lone pair-bond pair repulsion on the oxygen.

items.q: What does an increase in entropy of a system correspond to?
items.correct: An increase in the number of ways energy and particles can be arranged — typically a more dispersed/'disordered' state.
items.misconception: Entropy is just a measure of how messy a system looks visually.
items.random_wrong: Entropy measures how much energy a system contains in total.
items.why: The 'disorder' analogy is overused: students take it literally (mess vs tidy) instead of as a count of microstates.

items.q: What distinguishes a heterogeneous from a homogeneous catalyst?
items.correct: A heterogeneous catalyst is in a different phase from the reactants; a homogeneous catalyst is in the same phase.
items.misconception: Heterogeneous catalysts are solid and homogeneous catalysts are liquid — full stop.
items.random_wrong: Heterogeneous catalysts are consumed; homogeneous catalysts are not.
items.why: Students learn 'solid catalyst' examples (iron in Haber) and over-generalise — the actual distinction is about phase relative to the reactants, not the catalyst's physical state alone.

items.q: What is the common name for Group 1 metals?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Alkali metals. “Halogens” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which of these is a halogen?
items.why: Correct answer: Chlorine. “Sodium” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which group number contains the halogens?
items.why: The real figure is 7. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
